meta.name: Diabetic Ketoacidosis — Kussmaul Respirations and Profound Dehydration

patient.name: Allison Werner
patient.chiefComplaint: Progressive nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, weakness, and altered mental status in a known type-1 diabetic

history.hpi: 22-year-old female nursing student with a 12-year history of type-1 diabetes (insulin pump) developed a sore throat and low-grade fever 3 days ago and stopped eating. She continued some long-acting insulin (Tresiba) but reduced her pump bolus rates because she was 'not eating much,' and forgot to change the pump's infusion set 5 days ago — past the recommended 3-day change. Over the past 24 hours she developed progressive polyuria, polydipsia, nausea, several episodes of vomiting, diffuse mid-abdominal pain, and rapid deep breathing. Roommate found her this morning very weak, confused, with 'really fast deep breathing and her breath smelled weird.' Per home glucometer, blood glucose has been reading 'HIGH' (>500 mg/dL) for hours. No alcohol or drug use; no recent travel.
pastMedical: Type 1 diabetes mellitus (12 years, insulin pump for 7 years)
medications: Insulin pump: Humalog (rate reduced over past 3 days; infusion site not rotated for 5 days)
Insulin degludec (Tresiba) 12 units SQ at bedtime — last dose taken last night
history.lastOralIntake: Few sips of water this morning; minimal solid food x 3 days. Has vomited everything since last night.
history.events: 3-day viral illness with reduced PO intake and reduced insulin dosing; pump infusion set overdue for change. Progressive polyuria, polydipsia, vomiting, abdominal pain, and now altered mental status with rapid deep respirations.

scene.dispatch: Dispatch: 22-year-old female, altered mental status, rapid breathing. Diabetic per roommate.
scene.location: College apartment, 2nd floor, single bedroom. Patient lying in bed, dim room. Roommate at the door letting EMS in.
scene.safety: Scene secure. Roommate cooperative. Apartment is cluttered with school materials; bedroom has clear path to stretcher.
scene.bystanders: Roommate (primary historian) reports patient is a long-time type-1 diabetic with a pump. She has been sick with a cold for 3 days, vomiting since last night, and has been getting progressively weaker and breathing strangely all morning. Roommate found insulin pump still attached to the patient's abdomen.
visualCues: Patient supine in bed, eyes closed, very still
Visible insulin pump clipped to her waistband with infusion set on her abdomen
Empty water bottle and a 32 oz Gatorade on the nightstand
Vomit bucket at bedside with yellow-tinged emesis
Patient's lips and tongue are visibly dry and cracked
Distinct fruity / acetone breath odor noticeable from a few feet away
Slow, deep, sighing respirations visible across the room
Roommate hands EMS the home glucometer reading 'HI'

phases.name: Initial Contact
phases.description: EMS finds a 22-year-old type-1 diabetic in florid DKA: profound dehydration, Kussmaul respirations, fruity breath, altered mental status, mild compensated shock, and likely failed insulin pump infusion site as the trigger. The clinical picture is classic. This is the initial contact phase: dispatch, SAMPLE history, scene findings, and a focused metabolic assessment drive the response.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Patent and self-maintained; airway protective reflexes intact
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Slow, deep, sighing Kussmaul respirations; clear bilateral lung sounds; no wheezing or rales; obvious increased depth of inspiration
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Rapid, weak radial pulse; cap refill 3-4 sec; cool peripheries with mottled hands and feet
clinicalPresentation.pupils: Equal at 3 mm, reactive; eyes appear sunken
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Moves all extremities; weak grip strength bilaterally; no focal deficit
otherFindings: Dry mucous membranes; tongue dry and furrowed
Diffuse mid-abdominal tenderness without rigidity or rebound
Insulin pump still attached at right lower abdomen with infusion site mildly erythematous
Decreased urine output (per roommate, last urination was several hours ago despite drinking)
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: Slow, sleepy, slurred: 'My stomach hurts... so thirsty... so tired.'

expectedActions.shortAction: Recognize DKA: hyperglycemia + Kussmaul + fruity breath + dehydration
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Pertinent Assessment Findings: Concomitant trauma. Abdominal pain, 'fruity breath,' and rapid-deep respirations (Kussmaul respirations) may be associated with DKA.
expectedActions.rationale: Early recognition triggers fluids, EtCO2 monitoring, EKG for K+ changes, and appropriate destination.

expectedActions.shortAction: Confirm blood glucose (>250 mg/dL = treat per Hyperglycemia protocol)
expectedActions.rationale: Glucose >250 with symptoms is the entry point for the fluid resuscitation algorithm.

expectedActions.shortAction: Maintain airway; head of bed elevated if mental status declines further
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Closely monitor for signs of altered mental status, increased intracranial pressure, and immediately discontinue IV fluids and elevate head of bed if signs of increased ICP develop. Reassess and manage airway as needed.
expectedActions.rationale: Aspiration risk is high — vomiting, altered mental status, and ongoing insulin therapy.

expectedActions.shortAction: O2 to maintain SpO2 94-98% (currently 98%; nasal cannula adjunct)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Treatment and Interventions: If altered level of consciousness, stroke, or sepsis/septic shock, treat per Altered Mental Status Guideline, Suspected Stroke/Transient Ischemic Attack Guideline, or Shock Guideline accordingly.
expectedActions.rationale: SpO2 is fine; do not blunt respiratory drive (Kussmaul is compensatory). Monitor only.

expectedActions.shortAction: Establish two large-bore IVs for fluid resuscitation
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Establish IV access. If unable to obtain within two attempts or less than 90 seconds, place an IO needle.
expectedActions.rationale: Aggressive fluid resuscitation is the central DKA intervention; redundant access supports any deterioration.

expectedActions.shortAction: NS 20 mL/kg = 1160 mL bolus at 1000 mL/hr; reassess
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: If glucose greater than 250 mg/dL with symptoms of dehydration, vomiting, abdominal pain, or altered level of consciousness: Provide volume expansion with normal saline bolus. Adult: Normal saline 20 mL/kg at rate of 1000 mL/hr; if symptoms of hypovolemic shock, follow Shock Guideline.
expectedActions.rationale: DKA-specific protocol fluid dose; balances correction of dehydration with risk of cerebral edema (cerebral edema is rare in adults but still a concern).

expectedActions.shortAction: 12-lead EKG (look for hyperkalemia: peaked T waves)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Obtain 12-lead EKG to assess for findings consistent with hyperkalemia or acute coronary syndrome.
expectedActions.rationale: Insulin deficiency drives K+ out of cells; peaked T waves or QRS widening change management priorities.

expectedActions.shortAction: Place waveform EtCO2 (low values = severe acidosis)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Pulse oximetry and EtCO2 (reading of less than 25 mmHg may be sign of poor perfusion).
expectedActions.rationale: EtCO2 quantifies the metabolic acidosis severity and tracks response to fluids; <25 = sick.

expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Ondansetron (contraindicated for suspected or known diagnosis of prolonged QT syndrome). Adult: 4 mg IV/PO/SL OR 4 or 8 mg SL of the ODT formulation.
expectedActions.rationale: Reduces aspiration risk and improves patient comfort; check 12-lead for QT first.

expectedActions.shortAction: Do NOT give insulin in the field; do NOT remove pump (still delivering basal)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Performance Measures: When hyperglycemia documented, appropriate volume replacement given while avoiding overzealous repletion before insulin therapy at receiving center.
expectedActions.rationale: Field insulin without K+ monitoring risks fatal hypokalemia. Hospital starts insulin after K+ confirmed.

expectedActions.shortAction: Transport with notification (DKA, glucose, vitals, EtCO2)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Disposition: Transport to closest appropriate receiving facility.
expectedActions.rationale: Pre-arrival notification with metabolic status and trends primes the ED for fluids, electrolytes, and insulin drip.

transitions.if: Fluid bolus given; reassessing en route
transitions.condition: Crew has correctly identified DKA, established IV access, started a 20 mL/kg NS bolus at 1000 mL/hr, withheld field insulin, and is transporting with monitoring and pre-arrival notification.

Crew gave back-to-back rapid 1L pressure-bolus boluses without reassessment, exceeding recommended rate; patient develops worsening mental status concerning for cerebral edema (rare in adults but real with overly rapid correction). Crew must immediately stop fluids and elevate the head of bed.

phases.name: Fluids reassessed
phases.description: After 20 mL/kg of NS, the patient's perfusion is improving — heart rate trending down, EtCO2 trending up, and mental status is somewhat clearer. Glucose remains markedly elevated; correction is the hospital's job. Continued fluids at maintenance rate, transport ongoing.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew gave the protocol-specified 20 mL/kg NS bolus at 1000 mL/hr, monitored for response, and continued transport without inappropriate insulin or aggressive escalation.

clinicalPresentation.breathing: Still deep but slightly less labored Kussmaul; clear bilateral lung sounds
clinicalPresentation.circulation: Stronger radial pulse; cap refill improving to 2-3 sec; warmer hands
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Moves all extremities; following commands more reliably
otherFindings: Mucous membranes still dry but skin tenting improving
Patient now answering simple questions appropriately
clinicalPresentation.patientSpeech: 'Feeling a little better. Still really thirsty.'

expectedActions.shortAction: Reassess vitals + mental status q5min after each bolus
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Reassess patient: Reassess vital signs (pulse, blood pressure, respiratory rate, neurologic status assessment), mental status, and signs of dehydration. If mental status changes, reassess blood glucose level and provide appropriate treatment if hypoglycemia has developed.
expectedActions.rationale: Catches both inadequate fluid response and emerging cerebral edema or iatrogenic hypoglycemia.

expectedActions.shortAction: Watch for mental status worsening (cerebral edema warning)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Overly aggressive administration of fluid in hyperglycemic patients may cause cerebral edema or dangerous hyponatremia. Cerebral edema is a leading cause of death in children with DKA but is very rare in adults. Closely monitor for signs of altered mental status, increased intracranial pressure, and immediately discontinue IV fluids and elevate head of bed if signs of increased ICP develop.
expectedActions.rationale: Adult cerebral edema is rare but devastating; protocol explicitly requires monitoring.

expectedActions.shortAction: Continue cardiac monitor for K+ changes (peaked T or wide QRS)
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Obtain 12-lead EKG to assess for findings consistent with hyperkalemia or acute coronary syndrome.
expectedActions.rationale: K+ shifts during fluids are dynamic; EKG is the only field tool for detection.

phases.name: Cerebral edema concern
phases.description: After two rapid 1L pressure-bolus boluses without reassessment, the patient becomes more lethargic, harder to arouse, with a unilateral sluggish pupil and complaints of headache before becoming nonverbal. This is the rare-but-feared cerebral edema complication of overly rapid DKA correction. Crew must stop fluids, elevate the head of bed, manage airway, and notify receiving facility immediately.
phases.triggerCondition: Crew exceeded protocol fluid rate (e.g., 1 L pressure-infused, repeated x 2 without reassessment). Patient's mental status has worsened despite improving vitals — concerning for cerebral edema.

clinicalPresentation.airway: Becoming compromised — gag is sluggish, snoring respirations developing
clinicalPresentation.breathing: Kussmaul replaced by shallow, slowing respirations — concerning
clinicalPresentation.pupils: Right pupil 5 mm sluggish; left pupil 3 mm reactive
clinicalPresentation.motorFunction: Withdraws from pain; no purposeful movement to verbal stimuli
otherFindings: Patient complained of headache 5 minutes ago, then became nonverbal
One episode of vomiting after pupil change
Glasgow Coma Score has dropped 4 points in 10 minutes

expectedActions.shortAction: Stop the IV fluid bolus immediately; convert to TKO
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Closely monitor for signs of altered mental status, increased intracranial pressure, and immediately discontinue IV fluids and elevate head of bed if signs of increased ICP develop.
expectedActions.rationale: Recovery action — stopping ongoing fluid is the first protocol-mandated intervention for suspected cerebral edema.

expectedActions.shortAction: Elevate head of bed to reduce ICP
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Patient Safety Considerations: Closely monitor for signs of altered mental status, increased intracranial pressure, and immediately discontinue IV fluids and elevate head of bed if signs of increased ICP develop.
expectedActions.rationale: Promotes venous outflow from the cerebral venous system.

expectedActions.shortAction: Reassess airway; BVM if respirations <10 or SpO2 falling
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Reassess and manage airway as needed.
expectedActions.rationale: Cushing reflex and apnea may follow; have BVM ready and consider advanced airway preparation.

expectedActions.shortAction: Emergent notification: 'DKA with concern for cerebral edema'
expectedActions.protocolExcerpt: Disposition: Transport to closest appropriate receiving facility.
expectedActions.rationale: Pediatric/young-adult cerebral edema requires immediate ICU and possible mannitol/3% saline.
